---
title: "2.6 Comparing Boolean Expressions"
subtitle: "Part 2: Object, String, and null Comparisons"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit2-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Comparing Objects

### Different from primitive values

Comparing objects is a little different than comparing primitive typed values like numbers.

Objects can be very complex and have many attribute values or instance variables inside them.

When comparing two objects, we need a specially written <span class="term">equals</span> method to compare all of these values.

## String Equality

### `equals` compares letters

The `equals` method for Strings compares two strings letter by letter.

`s1.equals(s2)` is true if `s1` and `s2` have all the same characters in the same order.

With `String` objects and other objects, you almost always use `equals` instead of `==` to check their equality.

## Object References

### `==` compares references

When the operator `==` or `!=` is used to compare object variables, it returns true when the two variables <span class="mark">refer to the same object</span>.

These variables are called <span class="term">object references</span> and <span class="term">aliases</span> for the same object.

![String aliases](../../assets/2-selection-and-iteration/2-6-comparing-boolean-expressions/stringEquality.png){fig-align="center" width="38%"}

## Code Task

### `activecode:: lcse1`

Textbook prompt: If you run the following, what will be printed?

```java
public class Test1
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        String s1 = new String("Hello");
        String s2 = new String("Bye");
        String s3 = s2; // s3 is now an alias for s2
        System.out.println(s3);
        System.out.println(s2 == s3);
        System.out.println(s2.equals(s3));
    }
}
```

## Expected Output

### `activecode:: lcse1`

Runestone expects:

```text
Bye
true
true
```

Here is the representation of memory where `s2` and `s3` refer to the same String object.

![s2 and s3 are aliases referring to the same String object](../../assets/2-selection-and-iteration/2-6-comparing-boolean-expressions/s2ands3.jpg){fig-align="center" width="44%"}

## Equality with New Strings

### `new` creates a new object

If you use the `new` keyword to create a string, it will always create a new string object.

Even if two string objects created with `new` contain all the same characters in the same order, they will not refer to the same object.

## Code Task

### `activecode:: strEquals`

Textbook prompt: What will the following print? Run the code to see the difference between `==` and `equals` with new Strings that are both `"Hello"`.

Then, write the if statements described below to test the equality of `s1` and `s3` to see if capitalization matters.

## Code Window

### `activecode:: strEquals`

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class Test2
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        String s1 = new String("Hello");
        String s2 = new String("Hello");
        System.out.println(s1 == s2);
        System.out.println(s1.equals(s2));

        String s3 = new String("hello");
        // Write an if statement to test if
        //  s1 and s2 are equal with equals
        // If they are, print "Equals"

        // Write an if/else statement to test if
        //   s1 and s3 are equal with equals
        // If they are, print "Equals"
        // else print "Not Equals".
        // Note that case matters with equals unless you use equalsIgnoreCase

    }
}
```
:::

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: strEquals`

Runestone expects:

```text
false
true
Equals
Not Equals
```

It also checks that the code contains an `if` statement and at least `3` uses of `.equals`.

## Memory Representation

### Same letters, different objects

Since `new` was used, two different `String` objects are created with the characters `Hello`.

`s1 == s2` is false because they do not refer to the same object, but `s1.equals(s2)` is true since they contain the same characters in the same order.

![Two strings that are equal with equals but not with ==](../../assets/2-selection-and-iteration/2-6-comparing-boolean-expressions/s1ands2.jpg){fig-align="center" width="44%"}

## String Literal Note

### AP exam boundary

You can also create Strings using string literals instead of `new`, like:

```java
String s = "Hello";
```

String literals behave a little differently because they are re-used if they already exist instead of creating a new object. You should not see questions with string literals and `==` on the AP exam.

## String Comparison Rule

### Use `equals` for letter-by-letter equality

Only use `==` and `!=` with primitive types like `int` or to test if two strings or objects refer to the same object.

Use `equals`, not `==` or `!=`, with strings to test if they are equal letter by letter.

With other objects, use `equals` to see if all of their relevant attributes are equal.

## Quick Check

### `mchoice:: qsbeq_1`

Which of the following is true after the code executes?

```java
String s1 = new String("hi");
String s2 = new String("bye");
String s3 = new String("hi");
s2 = s1;
```

Options:

::: {.tight-list}
- `s1 == s2 && s1 == s3`
- `s1 == s2 && s1.equals(s3)`
- `s1 != s2 && s1.equals(s3)`
:::

Correct answer: `s1 == s2 && s1.equals(s3)`.

## Quick Check

### `mchoice:: qsbeq_2`

Which of the following is true after the code executes?

```java
String s1 = new String("hi");
String s2 = new String("bye");
String s3 = new String("hi");
```

Correct answer: `s1 != s3 && s1.equals(s3)`.

Reasoning: `s1` and `s3` refer to different string objects but they contain the same characters `"hi"` in the same order.

## Quick Check

### `mchoice:: qsbeq_3`

Which of the following is true after the code executes?

```java
String s1 = new String("hi");
String s2 = new String("bye");
String s3 = new String("hi");
```

Correct answer: `!(s1 == s2) && !(s1 == s3)`.

Reasoning: all of the variables refer to different objects. But `s1.equals(s3)` would be true since they have the same characters in the same order.

## Comparing with null

### Use `==` or `!=`

One common place to use `==` or `!=` with objects is to compare them to <span class="term">null</span> to see if they really exist.

Sometimes short-circuit evaluation is used to avoid an error if the object doesn't exist.

## Code Task

### `activecode:: nullTest`

Textbook prompt: Try the following code to see a `NullPointerException`. Since `s` is `null`, trying to access `indexOf` on `s` throws a `NullPointerException`.

Comment out the first `if` statement and run the program again. The second `if` statement avoids the error with short-circuit evaluation.

## Code Window

### `activecode:: nullTest`

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class NullTest
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        String s = null;
        if (s.indexOf("a") >= 0)
        {
            System.out.println(s + " contains an a");
        }
        if (s != null && s.indexOf("a") >= 0)
        {
            System.out.println(s + " contains an a");
        }
    }
}
```
:::

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: nullTest`

The task asks you to change `s` to set it to `"apple"` instead of `null` in the first line and run the code again.

Runestone expects:

```text
apple contains an a
apple contains an a
```

It also checks that the code has not removed `String s =` or the print statement.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- distinguish `==` reference comparison from `equals` content comparison
- identify aliases when two variables refer to the same object
- explain why two `new String("Hello")` objects are not `==`
- use `.equals` for String letter-by-letter comparison
- compare object references with `null` using `==` or `!=`
- use short-circuit evaluation to avoid calling a method on `null`
:::

## End

### Continue to Part 3

[Next: Truth and Tracing Tables POGIL →](2-6-part-3-truth-and-tracing-tables-pogil.html)
